methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $300M last post money.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in Climate & Energy. Range: $120M - $500M.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

private.name: Pano AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $500M+

private.name: Sage Geosystems
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $400M

private.name: Reverion
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $310M

private.name: Cemvita
private.rationale: Same sector (Climate & Energy), same stage (Series B), post-money $120M

data.narrative:
CarbonCapture Inc., a Series B climate and energy technology company founded by Adrian Corless, is evaluated against a peer universe of four same-stage private competitors (Pano AI, Sage Geosystems, Reverion, Cemvita) and one public comparable (LNZA). The company's current post-money valuation stands at $300M following $80M in total capital raised.

Two triangulation methods were applied. Stage-progression analysis, grounded in the $300M last post-money valuation and a typical Series B-to-next-stage multiplier of 1.9x, implies a forward valuation of $570M. Peer-median analysis across the four same-sector, same-stage comparables yielded a median valuation of $355M, with individual peer valuations ranging from $120M (Cemvita) to $500M (Pano AI). This four-company cohort reflects material heterogeneity in valuation outcomes within the climate and energy sector at Series B.

The two methods triangulate to a median valuation of $462M, with a low bound of $302M (near current valuation) and a high bound of $656M. The $360M spread between low and high estimates reflects meaningful uncertainty around growth trajectory, unit economics, and sector maturation timing. Stage-progression and peer comparables diverge by $215M, suggesting sensitivity to both the multiplier assumption and peer selection. Key uncertainties include technology differentiation relative to the peer cohort, capital efficiency relative to fund-raising needs, and regulatory environment for carbon capture deployment.
